title: Confiteor
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The shore-boat lies in the morning light,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ By the good ship ready for sailing;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The skies are clear, and the dawn is bright,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thoן¿½ the bar of the bay is fleckן¿½d with white,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the wind is fitfully wailing;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Near the tiller stands the priest, and the knight
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Leans over the quarter-railing.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½There is time while the vessel tarries still,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There is time while her shrouds are slack,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There is time ere her sails to the west wind fill,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ere her tall masts vanish from town and from hill,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ere cleaves to her keel the track:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There is time for confession to those who will,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To those who may never come back.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Sir priest, you can shrive these men of mine,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And, I pray you, shrive them fast,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And shrive those hardy sons of the brine,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Captain and mates of the Eglantine,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And sailors before the mast;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then pledge me a cup of the Cyprus wine,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For I fain would bury the past.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And hast thou naught to repent, my son?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Dost thou scorn confession and shrift?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ere thy sands from the glass of time shall run
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Is there naught undone that thou shouldן¿½st have done,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Naught done that thou shouldן¿½st have left?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The guiltiest soul may from guilt be won,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the stoniest heart may be cleft.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Have my ears been closed to the prayer of the poor,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or deaf to the cry of distress?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Have I given little, and taken more?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Have I brought a curse to the widowן¿½s door?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Have I wrongן¿½d the fatherless?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Have I steepן¿½d my fingers in guiltless gore,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That I must perforce confess?ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Have thy steps been guided by purity
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Through the paths with wickedness rife?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Hast thou never smitten thine enemy?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Hast thou yielded naught to the lust of the eye,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And naught to the pride of life?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Hast thou passן¿½d all snares of pleasure by?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Hast thou shunnן¿½d all wrath and strife?ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Nay, certes! a sinful life Iן¿½ve led,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Yet Iן¿½ve suffered, and lived in hope;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I may suffer still, but my hope has fled,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Iן¿½ve nothing now to hope or to dread,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And with fate I can fairly cope;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Were the waters closing over my head,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I should scarcely catch at a rope.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Dost suffer? thy pain may be fraught with grace,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Since never by works alone
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We are saved; the penitent thief may trace
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The wealth of love in the Saviourן¿½s face
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To the Pharisee rarely shown;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the Magdaleneן¿½s arms may yet embrace
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The foot of the jasper throne.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Sir priest, a heavier doom I dree,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For I feel no quickening pain,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But a dull, dumb weight when I bow my knee,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And (not with the words of the Pharisee)
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ My hard eyes heavenward strain,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Where my dead darling prayeth for me!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Now, I wot, she prayeth in vain!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Still I hear it over the battleן¿½s din,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And over the festive cheer,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So she prayן¿½d with claspן¿½d hands, white and thin,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The prayer of a soul absolved from sin,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For a soul that is dark and drear,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For the light of repentance bursting in,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the flood of the blinding tear.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Say, priest! when the saint must vainly plead,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh! how shall the sinner fare?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I hold your comfort a broken reed;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Let the witherן¿½d branch for itself take heed,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ While the green shoots wait your care;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Iן¿½ve striven, though feebly, to grasp your creed,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Iן¿½ve grappled my own despair.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½By the little within thee, good and brave,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Not wholly shattered, though shaken;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ By the soul that crieth beyond the grave,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The love that He once in His mercy gave,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In His mercy since retaken,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I conjure thee, oh! sinner, pardon crave,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I implore thee, oh! sleeper, waken!ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Go to! shall I lay my black soul bare
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To a vain, self-righteous man?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In my sin, in my sorrow, you may not share,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And yet could I meet with one who must bear
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The load of an equal ban,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With him I might strive to blend one prayer,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The wail of the Publican.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½My son, I, too, am a withered bough,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ My place is to others given;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thou hast sinnן¿½d, thou sayest; I ask not how,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For I, too, have sinnן¿½d, even as thou,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And I, too, have feebly striven,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And with thee I must bow, crying, ן¿½Shrive us now!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Our Father which art in heaven!ן¿½ן¿½
meaning:
This evocative piece by Adam Lindsay Gordon represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Adam Lindsay Gordon's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
